context: Derived from repo MSP provider SOP and public guidance source spine: CISA AA22-131A, CISA MSP risk considerations, NSA/CISA/MS-ISAC RMM guidance, NCSC MSP/cloud/secure-admin guidance, and NIST SP 800-61r3.
text: MSP provider operating invariants: Tenant separation is a hard boundary: use one internal major incident record plus per-tenant records for evidence, approvals, impact, and communications. A suspected MSP control plane becomes evidence, not truth; corroborate RMM, PSA, backup portal, cloud admin, identity, remote access, or vendor plugin data with endpoint, SIEM, EDR, firewall, IdP, storage-side, database/file-system, or vendor-side evidence. Preserve evidence before cleanup when feasible: logs, package/script content, job history, command lines, identities, tokens, source IPs, timestamps, screenshots/exports, hashes, and chain-of-custody. Stop spread with the narrowest safe control. Disruptive actions need gates: evidence captured or emergency exception, blast-radius confidence, business impact, customer-owner approval where required, senior authority, rollback, and monitoring. Customer communications must be tenant-specific and should not reveal other affected customers.
title: MSP provider operating invariants

context: Derived from repo MSP provider SOP and public guidance source spine: CISA AA22-131A, CISA MSP risk considerations, NSA/CISA/MS-ISAC RMM guidance, NCSC MSP/cloud/secure-admin guidance, and NIST SP 800-61r3.
text: MSP major incident first-hour SOP: Declare posture first: suspicious activity, security incident, major incident, or emergency. If the event is multi-tenant, destructive, privileged, regulated, ransomware-like, or control-plane related, escalate early. Establish command outside suspect systems when needed, open an internal bridge and scribe log, create the internal major incident record, then create per-tenant child records. Assign incident lead, technical lead, evidence owner, customer-success/comms owner, and vendor liaison where possible. Preserve console state, audit logs, system logs, package/script content, job queue, task definitions, endpoint process tree, command lines, network connections, identity/session logs, API token use, source IPs, timestamps, and approvals. Hash exports and record collector, method, source, and time. Stop spread narrowly: freeze job/package/token/session/route/policy before broad isolation unless active harm demands emergency action. Communicate internally with full scope; communicate externally only with tenant-specific facts, uncertainty, actions, approvals needed, and next update time.
title: MSP major incident first-hour SOP

context: Derived from repo MSP provider SOP and public guidance source spine: CISA AA22-131A, CISA MSP risk considerations, NSA/CISA/MS-ISAC RMM guidance, NCSC MSP/cloud/secure-admin guidance, and NIST SP 800-61r3.
text: MSP provider security baseline: Maintain a contractual responsibility matrix for hardening, monitoring, incident response, recovery, customer notifications, evidence access, and subcontractors. Require MFA for MSP accounts accessing customer environments, with phishing-resistant MFA for high-risk admin functions where possible. Use least privilege, named accounts, no credential reuse across customers, prompt deprovisioning, and periodic review of stale customer access and infrastructure. Segregate customer data and services from each other and from internal MSP systems where possible. Keep central logging with appropriate customer visibility, retention, alerting, and auditability of MSP activity. Prioritize patching known exploited vulnerabilities. Test backups and recovery paths, including offline or isolated backups and protected encryption keys. Exercise incident response and recovery plans, including offline access. Track vendor/subcontractor risk for plugins, integrations, delegated providers, and update channels.
title: MSP provider security baseline

context: Derived from repo MSP provider SOP and public guidance source spine: CISA AA22-131A, CISA MSP risk considerations, NSA/CISA/MS-ISAC RMM guidance, NCSC MSP/cloud/secure-admin guidance, and NIST SP 800-61r3.
text: MSP scenario gates: RMM/remote access abuse requires auditing installed tools, preventing unauthorized RMM execution, using approved access paths, freezing suspect automation, and preserving scripts, packages, jobs, agent logs, operator accounts, API tokens, command lines, endpoint process data, and network evidence. Backup or destructive-job incidents require treating green dashboards and successful logins as insufficient proof, preserving portal session/job/API/audit/source IP/identity evidence, maintaining active restores, and verifying immutability from storage-side evidence. Identity/OAuth/admin-session incidents require preserving sign-in logs, OAuth grants, forwarding rules, API use, admin changes, source IPs, device posture, and MFA events before or alongside revocation. Service-change pressure requires validating authority, ticket evidence, tenant, site, VLAN/subnet, DNS, firewall scope, dependency, change window, rollback, and monitoring; hold only the unsafe slice when safe partial progress exists.
title: MSP scenario gates for RMM backup identity and service changes
